reasoning: The AJAX handler mk_dp_close_dp_help() is registered only under wp_ajax_ (line 44), excluding unauthenticated users. The nonce 'nc_help_desk' is generated exclusively in custom_assets() (line 434), which is called only from inc/admin-settings.php (line 4). That include is only loaded by duplicate_page_settings() (line 100), which itself is gated behind current_user_can('manage_options') (line 99) and registered as an options sub-page with the 'manage_options' capability (line 91). WordPress enforces the capability check before rendering the page, so only Administrators can access it and obtain the nonce. A subscriber-level (or any non-admin) logged-in user is redirected by WordPress before the settings page renders; they cannot receive the wp_localize_script output containing the nonce. The nonce itself is a standard WordPress 10-character pseudorandom token (cryptographically unguessable) and is time-limited. There is no other code path in the plugin — no REST endpoint, no public frontend hook, no wp_ajax_nopriv_ handler — that emits or leaks the 'nc_help_desk' nonce to non-admins. Therefore, the prerequisite for exploitation (possession of a valid 'nc_help_desk' nonce) requires Administrator-level access, meaning the attacker already has the same or higher trust level than the victim. The finding is not default-exploitable by low-privilege authenticated users as the original reachability claim assumed. Severity is downgraded to INFORMATIONAL.
